In this lesson, we're going to talk about the last critical piece before you start finding your leads and contacting them. That step is creating your deal team and lock them down so you're ready to actually do a deal. A deal team consists of a business broker, an accountant, a lawyer, and a commercial real estate agent. Sometimes, your business broker is also a real estate agent. We'll cover each of these roles in a little bit, but there are three steps to putting together your deal team. Step one, identify an M&A lead advisor, someone who can help you with the merger or acquisition that has experience. Step two, identify supporting professional advisors. Usually an M&A lead advisor will know another advisor that they've already worked with. And step three, drive the deal team to attack with speed. Remember, a lot of accountants, a lot of lawyers, and a lot of deal people, they're not really used to attack speed. They kind of go with the flow, so you're going to have to commit to pushing them if you want to get a deal done. Since everything flows from finding a lead M&A advisor, let's start with that position. This lead advisor needs to have had multiple experiences in buying and selling businesses in the past. He or she should have done the types of deals that you're trying to do and can show you by example of past deals, their experience. When you're talking to your lead advisor or anyone else on the deal team, you need to ask the best question possible, which is, what's the biggest successful business acquisition you've been involved in that's relative to what I'm trying to do? If they say they haven't worked on a business like the one you're trying to buy, then don't put them on your deal team. Equally, if they haven't done any kind of business that's in the range you're trying to buy, don't put them on your deal team. If they're new and they don't know what they're doing, don't put them on your deal team. Now, you're looking for a deal team that's done at or bigger deals than the one you're trying to do. That way, they can help you see opportunities and pitfalls that you have not experienced yet. Once you have your ideal lead advisor candidate, there are two methods you can use to lock them in. If you can afford it, put them on retainer and pay them as you go along. Otherwise, if you identify a great deal, you can modify that by offering them a piece of the deal for helping you get it closed. Or if you prefer keeping your deal process to a little to no money out of pocket, then you can make a deal with an advisor where you'll pay them a bigger fee once the deal gets closed, anchoring them into having to follow through with you getting the deal closed. That'll give them the inspiration to help you all the way through it. The lead advisor of your deal team must be someone with experience in mergers and acquisitions to ensure your deal is properly structured. I have seen horrible structures in the marketplace. Make sure you do your homework here. Too often, I've seen buyers delegate this role to a lawyer which results in unnecessary delays, more drama, and believe it or not, to their advantage, more money. You end up paying them for more time because they extend how much time it takes to get a deal done. Do not make the same mistake. I've made this mistake over and over. I've learned to put my deal team on a budget and either get them to buy in and they can do it because they're experts or they won't be on that team. Then step two, you need to find your lawyer or an accountant or and or lawyer and accountant in a commercial real estate agent. Most small business brokers have a commercial real estate license as well. If they have that, then you don't need that person. Here's the other thing. If you find an advisor that you like that's done lots of transactions, this is just a tip, they usually have these other relationships they can bring to you. The commercial real estate agent is there for lead generation. Trust me, they always know what's going on in the business marketplace because they're looking at the leases and people who are extending, not extending, thinking of selling their businesses or their buildings because a business owner will call and say, hey, I think I'm going to sell my business so will you list my building? They know in advance what's going on. They'll be able to tell you which businesses are winding down if their leases are coming up and they're not going to renew it. You get intel from those people. Start finding these candidates by asking your lead advisor if they have names that they know are competent and trusted. If not, begin your outreach for each and ask your lead advisor to help craft the criteria they should meet given your vision for the type of businesses you're looking for. Look, if you're looking to buy a restaurant, you should go to an agent that deals in restaurants. If you're looking to buy some kind of business, some other kind of business, find an expert that serves those specific businesses or has experience underneath their belt. Just like your lead advisor, you can pay them as you go along or you can give them a succession fee. When the deal closes, they get paid. That means they're going to be pulling for you to get the deal closed. One other tip, they may want you to close a deal that maybe you shouldn't be buying so they can get paid. You've got to balance out the guidance and advice you're getting with the intention and the agenda of the people helping you. Higher quality talent is typically paid a percentage of the transaction. It could be if it's a business broker or if it's a commercial broker that brings you a business, it could be a brokerage fee of 3% to 10%. To hire a middle tier valuation accountant, it could be an initial setup and retainer of 5,000 to 10,000 and the same with an attorney. You can spend a lot of money putting that team in place. Being very clear about what you're wanting to buy and finding the right people is critically important. Total legal fees could be between 10,000 and 20,000 for just a reasonable average business and up to $100,000 if there's complexity in the deal. That's because the more complexity an acquisition is, the more tax considerations, the better legal advice you need in the deal. Don't be afraid of this though because if the deal is good enough, the money invested is a great, great asset. But don't be afraid of this because if the deal is good enough, you're going to have to learn to invest money into your business. You might as well start at the beginning. If you're able, you could try striking a deal with your team members on a success fee and a retainer, a combination of the both because that way you show that you're committed and you're willing up front and they're going to know you're serious about trying to do a deal on the back end. Great advisors might not accept this structure, so be aware of that. The bigger they are and the busier they are, they equally may be like, hey, it's too small a deal and it's not enough money on the front end. You need to send the message that you're serious about getting the deal done. All right, so where are we at? We know what kind of business we want to buy. We've interviewed and hired team members who can help us find those businesses. Now you might take a moment and ask them to assess your strengths and weaknesses. Be as specific as possible with your time frame, your expectations and work with your deal team because now you're going to 10X your activity to go out with a tax speed and find those deals that are waiting for you in your market. And remember, the speed in which you think is the speed in which you act. The faster you can think, the faster you can act. The more clear you are about what you want, the faster you can act. The faster you reach your goals, the more confidence you'll have. You need to reduce the drag time between thinking and acting to give your deal team confidence that you're serious. Your deal team is there to help you speed up your thinking based on their experience and their expertise. But you need to drive that team by making your goals and the attack speed in which you're willing to go after them, your number one mission. If you're distracted, your deal team will get distracted. If you're not serious, your deal team won't be serious. You need to take ownership over this process. This is different from simply stating the outcome you want. Stating the outcome you want is uninspiring and it's a me message. Me, me, me, me, me, me, me. I know one wants to listen to that. Instead of the me message, you want a we message, a true mission, communicated and driven well that inspires the team and ensures there's alignment towards the goal because if it's a shared reward, it must be a shared passion, a shared attempt. If you're sitting there trying to get your people to do things for you and you're unwilling to do it, they will not find you a deal because they will know inherently that you won't know what to do with the deal if you do get one because you're not leading from the front. As an acquisition entrepreneur, it's your number one responsibility as you symbol your team, you start picking the verticals you're going after and you start opening communications up with targeted businesses to buy to be crystal clear about the vision and the outcomes that you've established for this process. The more you can do that, the more sophisticated of an acquisition entrepreneur you become and it is our intention in this program to teach you the skills to be a sophisticated acquisition entrepreneur.
